events.text: Patient is a 34-year-old African American male presenting with fatigue, easy bruising, and gingival bleeding over the past three weeks.
At age 34, the patient was diagnosed with acute myeloid leukemia NOS, ICD-O morphology code 9861/3, primary site bone marrow C42.1, after presenting with leukocytosis and circulating blasts.

At age 34, initial complete blood count revealed a white blood cell count of 112,000/uL with 68% blasts, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, and platelet count of 22,000/uL. Coagulation studies showed fibrinogen 98 mg/dL, D-dimer elevated at 14.2 ug/mL, and PT 16.8 seconds, concerning for disseminated intravascular coagulation.

At age 34, bone marrow biopsy with aspirate demonstrated a markedly hypercellular marrow (95% cellularity) with 82% myeloblasts by morphology. Blasts were medium to large with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, fine chromatin, and prominent nucleoli. Auer rods were identified in scattered blasts. Flow cytometry showed blasts positive for CD34, CD117, CD33, CD13, HLA-DR, and MPO, consistent with acute myeloid leukemia.

At age 34, next-generation sequencing panel on bone marrow aspirate identified FLT3-ITD mutation with an allelic ratio of 0.72, NPM1 mutation (type A insertion), and DNMT3A R882H mutation. No TP53, RUNX1, or ASXL1 mutations were detected.

At age 34, conventional cytogenetic analysis revealed a normal male karyotype, 46,XY, in all 20 metaphases analyzed. FISH studies were negative for PML-RARA, CBFB rearrangement, and KMT2A rearrangement.

At age 34, ELN 2022 risk stratification classified this patient as intermediate risk based on the co-occurrence of FLT3-ITD and NPM1 mutations with a normal karyotype. The presence of DNMT3A mutation was noted as an additional adverse prognostic factor.

At age 34, the patient was started on induction chemotherapy with the 7+3 regimen consisting of continuous infusion cytarabine 200 mg/m2 daily for seven days plus daunorubicin 60 mg/m2 on days one through three, combined with midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days eight through twenty-one.

At age 34, during induction the patient developed tumor lysis syndrome with uric acid rising to 12.4 mg/dL, potassium 6.1 mEq/L, phosphorus 7.8 mg/dL, and calcium 6.9 mg/dL, requiring aggressive intravenous hydration and rasburicase administration.

At age 34, the patient developed neutropenic fever on day nine of induction with an absolute neutrophil count of 0 and temperature of 39.4 degrees Celsius. Blood cultures grew Pseudomonas aeruginosa and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics were initiated with cefepime and later escalated to meropenem.
At age 34, the patient experienced grade 3 oral mucositis requiring IV morphine patient-controlled analgesia and total parenteral nutrition for ten days during the aplastic phase of induction.
At age 34, day 14 bone marrow biopsy showed a markedly hypocellular marrow (approximately 10% cellularity) with 3% residual blasts, consistent with a good early response to induction chemotherapy.
At age 34, recovery blood counts demonstrated an absolute neutrophil count above 1,000/uL and platelet count above 100,000/uL by day 32 of induction.

At age 34, day 28 bone marrow biopsy confirmed complete remission with 1.8% blasts by morphology and recovering trilineage hematopoiesis. Flow cytometry-based measurable residual disease assessment detected 0.04% residual leukemic cells with the original aberrant immunophenotype.

At age 34, consolidation cycle one was initiated with high-dose cytarabine 3 g/m2 every twelve hours on days one, three, and five, combined with midostaurin 50 mg orally twice daily on days eight through twenty-one.

At age 34, during consolidation the patient developed invasive pulmonary aspergillosis confirmed by positive serum galactomannan antigen (index 3.2) and CT chest showing a cavitary nodule with halo sign. Voriconazole was initiated with dose adjustment for midostaurin interaction.

At age 34, the patient proceeded to allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplant in first complete remission using a ten-of-ten HLA-matched unrelated donor with myeloablative conditioning consisting of busulfan and cyclophosphamide.
At age 34, GVHD prophylaxis consisted of tacrolimus and short-course methotrexate. Neutrophil engraftment occurred on day plus 14 and platelet engraftment on day plus 19.

At age 34, the patient developed grade 2 acute graft-versus-host disease involving the skin (stage 2 maculopapular rash over 40% body surface area) and upper gastrointestinal tract (persistent nausea and anorexia), treated with systemic corticosteroids.

At age 34, day plus 100 bone marrow biopsy showed complete donor chimerism at 99.2% by short tandem repeat analysis with no evidence of measurable residual disease by multiparameter flow cytometry (sensitivity 0.01%).
At age 34, PET-CT performed at day plus 100 post-transplant showed no FDG-avid disease and resolution of the previously noted pulmonary aspergillosis lesion with residual scarring.
At age 34, the patient was tapered off immunosuppression by day plus 180 and remained in complete molecular remission with negative FLT3-ITD by PCR at last follow-up.

scenario_blurb: Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with FLT3-ITD mutation. ELN intermediate/adverse risk. Induction with 7+3 plus midostaurin. Some achieving CR, some refractory. Include cytogenetics (normal karyotype or complex), blast percentage monitoring, allogeneic stem cell transplant in first remission for eligible patients.
